hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — 3, J. You raised aggressively before the flop, putting pressure on your opponent, who decided to call and see the community cards.
Flop — King, 10, 9. You bet to test your opponent's resolve, and they called again, still in the dark.
Turn — 3. You bet once more, trying to build the pot, and your opponent called for the second time, showing remarkable patience.
River — Queen. The board paired one of your opponent's hole cards, and they suddenly led out with a confident bet, turning the tables on you.

wrongReasons: 8 10 is close, but the river lead is the tell. The correct hand picked up more on the river than 8 10 would have.
J 7 makes a straight here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
6 7 has little reason to lead the river here — high card can't comfortably go for value after calling two streets.
hand.explanation:
Answer: Q 5

Your opponent called two streets with nothing but overcards, then the river card paired one of their hole cards, giving them One Pair. The sudden lead on the river — after calling twice — is the tell: they finally hit something and went for value on the River.
